did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a self-supervised Vision Transformer (ViT-S/16) trained with the DINO framework to classify multi-class skin cancer using the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. The model was fine-tuned and evaluated against CNN baselines (ResNet50, EfficientNet, Xception) for skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, including a proposed self-supervised Vision Transformer model (DINO-ViT-S/16), methodology for handling class imbalance, and quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) on the HAM10000 dataset. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICECTE 2026), it reports new technical contributions and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhancing Multi-class Skin Cancer Image Classification Using Self-supervised Vision Transformer Architecture
journal: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Computer & Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE 2026)
brief_summary: This study addresses multi-class skin cancer classification using a self-supervised Vision Transformer (ViT-S/16) trained with the DINO framework to overcome the limitations of traditional CNNs in capturing global context and handling data imbalance. The model was fine-tuned on the highly imbalanced HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset, employing dynamic on-the-fly augmentation and class-weighted sampling to mitigate overfitting. Evaluation was conducted using stratified 5-fold cross-validation to ensure robust and reproducible performance metrics. The proposed DINO-ViT-S/16 model achieved an accuracy of 95.86%, precision of 95.30%, recall of 95.44%, and F1-score of 95.88%. These results demonstrate superior performance compared to baseline models including ResNet50, EfficientNet, Xception, and Swin-Tiny, highlighting the effectiveness of self-supervised learning for dermatological image analysis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class classification. The paper notes it had 65 samples in the validation set with 59 correctly classified.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic classes of skin cancer (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs malignant or specific lesion types) using a Softmax output layer. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC curves.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image collection containing over 10,000 images categorized into seven diagnostic classes, used as a standard benchmark for testing Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) systems for skin cancer.

use.training: Used for training the self-supervised DINO-ViT-S/16 model and baseline models (ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, Xception, Swin-Tiny) via stratified 5-fold cross-validation (80% of data allocated to training folds).
use.validation: Used for validation via stratified 5-fold cross-validation (20% of data allocated to validation folds) to preserve class ratios and evaluate robustness.
use.testing: Evaluated using the validation splits from the 5-fold cross-validation; no separate held-out test set was reported.
use.other: Used for benchmarking and comparison against traditional CNN and Vision Transformer baselines.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure robust and reproducible evaluation of the model's performance by preserving class ratios across training and validation folds, addressing the issue of poor reliability from simple train-test splits.
To summarize the performance metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score) across the five folds of cross-validation, providing an estimate of the model's central tendency and variability.
To evaluate per-class performance, specifically identifying correct classifications and misclassifications among the seven skin cancer classes, confirming robust learning despite class imbalance.
To assess the separability of the classifier for different classes, particularly high-risk lesions like melanoma and BCC, by examining the trade-off between true positive and false positive rates.
To mitigate severe class imbalance during training by assigning weights inversely proportional to class frequency, ensuring proportionally higher contributions from minority classes.
To increase training diversity and reduce overfitting for minority classes by generating unique variants until they reached a minimum effective sample size per epoch.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation across 5-fold cross-validation) and comparative metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) against baseline models.

HAM10000.notes: Diagonal dominance confirms robust learning despite class imbalance. Misclassifications were few and occurred into visually similar lesion types.

architecture_used.pretraining: Self-supervised pretraining using the Distillation with NO labels (DINO) framework with teacher-student self-distillation strategy.

architecture_details.patch_embedding: Image divided into 16x16 patches, flattened, and projected linearly into a fixed dimension.
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Multi-head self-attention to capture long-range dependencies and global structural information.
architecture_details.self_distillation: Student network aligns with teacher network's soft predictions using Kullback-Leibler divergence (L_DINO = KL(P_t(x) || P_s(x))).

training_details.loss_function: Weighted categorical cross-entropy (L_WCE = - Σ w_c y_c log(ŷ_c))
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Image-weighting mechanism (weights inversely proportional to class frequency) and dynamic on-the-fly augmentation for classes with fewer than 1000 samples.
training_details.augmentation: Random horizontal/vertical flips, rotations (±30°), random zooming, brightness/contrast adjustments, affine transformations, Gaussian noise.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by Softmax
classification_or_output_head.hidden_layer: Fully connected layer with 512 neurons and ReLU activation
classification_or_output_head.output_classes: 7 (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of pixel values to the [0, 1] range
Image-weighting mechanism (weights inversely proportional to class frequency) applied during batch formation to mitigate class imbalance
Dynamic on-the-fly augmentation for classes with fewer than 1000 samples to ensure at least 1000 effective images per epoch

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to classes with fewer than 1000 samples to ensure at least 1000 effective images per epoch. Generated unique variants each epoch to reduce overfitting.

conclusions: The proposed self-supervised DINO-ViT-S/16 model achieved superior performance (95.86% accuracy, 95.30% precision, 95.44% recall, 95.88% F1-score) compared to traditional CNNs (ResNet50, EfficientNet, Xception) and Vision Transformer baselines (Swin-Tiny) on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset.

Self-supervised learning using the DINO framework enables Vision Transformers to learn powerful feature representations from dermatological images with limited labeled data, overcoming the need for large annotated datasets typical of supervised learning.

The combination of dynamic on-the-fly augmentation and image-weighting (class-weighted sampling) effectively mitigated class imbalance and reduced overfitting risks associated with minority classes in the HAM10000 dataset.
Stratified 5-fold cross-validation provided robust and reproducible performance evaluation, addressing the reliability issues often found in studies using simple train-test splits.

The model demonstrated strong generalization ability and low misclassification rates despite the inherent class imbalance, with high-risk lesions (Melanoma and BCC) showing steep ROC rises indicating low false-positive rates.

Future work should focus on training with larger, more varied clinical datasets, optimizing the framework for real-time deployment, and integrating explainable AI techniques to enhance trust and interpretability in clinical settings.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance (some classes <200 samples) and potential data leakage due to multiple images from the same patient; no external validation on independent clinical datasets was performed.

Performance metrics are reported as mean values across 5-fold cross-validation without providing standard deviations or confidence intervals for the final reported accuracy (95.86%) and other metrics, limiting assessment of statistical significance.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm that the performance gains over baselines (ResNet50, EfficientNet, Xception, Swin-Tiny) are statistically significant rather than due to variance.

Evaluation was conducted using stratified 5-fold cross-validation on the HAM10000 dataset; however, the specific fold-level standard deviations for the primary metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1) are not explicitly detailed in the summary text, only in Table III for the proposed model.

The model was trained on dermoscopic images only; clinical relevance is limited as the model has not been tested on non-dermoscopic clinical photographs or real-world clinical workflows with varying image quality and lighting conditions.

No explainable AI (XAI) techniques were implemented or reported, which limits the interpretability of the model's decisions and hinders trust for clinical deployment, as acknowledged in the future work section.

The study addresses class imbalance via dynamic on-the-fly augmentation and image-weighting, but does not discuss potential risks of overfitting to augmented minority class features or generalization to unseen minority class presentations.

The paper is a conference proceeding (ICECTE 2026) with limited scope compared to journal publications; the computational cost, inference time, and resource requirements for real-time deployment are not analyzed.
